coredata.dc:title: A dual-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor of biogenic amines with dye encapsulated covalent organic framework for meat freshness
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zexi
Li, Chenghui
He, Juan
Hou, Xiandeng
coredata.dc:description: Developing sensitive, nondestructive and real-time methods for assessing meat freshness is vital for ensuring food safety. Traditional monochromatic fluorescent probes for monitoring meat freshness markers like biogenic amines often face challenges in sensitivity, interference, visualization and portability for practical applications. Herein, we constructed a new Ru-Flu@COF fluorescence sensor, by combining a green fluorescent dye-encapsulated covalent organic framework (Flu@COF) with a red fluorescent compound (Ru (bpy)3 2+), exhibiting a dual inverse ratiometric fluorescence signal towards essential biogenic amines His and NH3·H2O, thus achieving a rich color gradient and spoilage-sensitive color discrimination. The detection linear range for His and NH3·H2O was 0.6–200 μM and 0.5–60 mg/L, with limits of detection of 0.5 μM and 0.2 mg/L, respectively. Furthermore, Ru-Flu@COF gel labels together with a smartphone enable portable, in situ and naked-eye monitoring freshness of pork, fish and shrimp, where the freshness level can be visually identified through the gel label color from red (fresh) to orange (less fresh), yellow (spoilage) and green (severe spoilage). The proposed smart label has the advantages of high sensitivity, excellent visual discrimination and portability, and it can be a promising platform for in situ nondestructive monitoring freshness of various kinds of meat for food safety inspection in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Biogenic amines
Visual sensing
In-situ monitoring
Meat freshness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of BAs and VOAs
Application for testing of meat freshness of real samples
Construction of amine-response ratiometric fluorescence sensing system
Sensing performance of Ru-Flu@COF system for amine
Visual sensing of volatile organic amines (VOAs)
Real-time in-situ visual inspection of real food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22204110), the Ministry of Education of China through 111 Project (No. B17030), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China. We also thank Dr. Yunfei Tian, Yanju Luo from the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the XPS and the fluorescence decay curves measurements.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22204110), the the 111 Center (B17030), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China. We also thank Dr. Yunfei Tian, Yanju Luo from the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the XPS and the fluorescence decay curves measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis scheme of the Ru-Flu@COF system. (b) Schematic diagram the Ru-Flu@COF hydrogel for real-time monitoring of meat freshness. Notes: PTSA: p-toluenesulfonic acid monohydrate; TpDq COF is the covalent organic framework (COF) used in this work, constructed by 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol monomer (TFP) and 2,6-diaminoanthraquinone monomer (DAAQ); Flu@COF refers to the material after encapsulation of sodium fluorescein in a TpDq COF.

SEM images of (A) COF and (B) Flu@COF. (C) PXRD spectra of simulated COF, DAAQ, TFP, experimentally synthesized COF and Flu@COF. (D) FT-IR spectra of DAAQ, TFP, COF, Flu and Flu@COF. (E) XPS full spectra of COF and Flu@COF. (F) UV–Vis spectra of TpDq COF, Flu@COF, Flu and Ru (bpy)3 2+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Flu@COF, Ru (bpy)3 2+ and Ru-Flu@COF systems in aqueous solution (insets a, b, c show the fluorescence images of the three systems under 302 nm UV light). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ru-Flu@COF at different His concentrations (insets show the fluorescence images of Ru-Flu@COF system with His concentrations of 0 μM, 50 μM and 200 μM). (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of Ru-Flu@COF systems at different His concentrations. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra and (E) fluorescence intensity ratio (F521/F601) of Ru-Flu@COF at different His concentrations. (G) Fluorescence emission spectra and (H) fluorescence intensity ratio (F521/F601) of Ru-Flu@COF at different NH3·H2O concentrations. The corresponding linear relationship between F521/F601 and (F) His and (I) NH3·H2O concentrations.

(A) Schematic diagram of gas-phase sensing of volatile amines in agarose hydrogels. (B) Fluorescence change of hydrogels after sensing with different concentrations of NH3·H2O. (C) Scatter plot of G/R values versus NH3·H2O concentration and (D) the linear relationship between them.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) Flu@COF, (B) Ru (bpy)3 2+ and (C) Ru-Flu@COF before and after the addition of 100 μM His (The insets were fluorescence images under 302 nm UV light). (D) Schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism.

(A) Schematic of monitoring shrimp freshness using the hydrogel labels and a smartphone. (B) Pictures of shrimp samples under natural light and 302 nm UV light after being stored at 25 °C, 4 °C and −20 °C for 48 h, 72 h and 5 d, respectively. (C) Line plots of G/R values of samples at 25 °C, 4 °C and −20 °C versus storage time. (D) Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content of the shrimp samples stored for different times at 25 °C, 4 °C and −20 °C. (E) Degree of linear correlation between T-VBN content and G/R value of shrimp. (F) Color card of the fluorescent color change of labels in relation to total VBNs content of shrimps. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of the method developed in this experiment with other literature methods for the detection of His and NH3·H2O.

entry.cross-ref: Zhang, Sun, Ma, Qin, and Tang (2023)

head.ce:title: A dual-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor of biogenic amines with dye encapsulated covalent organic framework for meat freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology, Ministry of Education, and College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology
Ministry of Education, and College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology, Ministry of Education, and College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing sensitive, nondestructive and real-time methods for assessing meat freshness is vital for ensuring food safety. Traditional monochromatic fluorescent probes for monitoring meat freshness markers like biogenic amines often face challenges in sensitivity, interference, visualization and portability for practical applications. Herein, we constructed a new Ru-Flu@COF fluorescence sensor, by combining a green fluorescent dye-encapsulated covalent organic framework (Flu@COF) with a red fluorescent compound (Ru (bpy)3 2+), exhibiting a dual inverse ratiometric fluorescence signal towards essential biogenic amines His and NH3·H2O, thus achieving a rich color gradient and spoilage-sensitive color discrimination. The detection linear range for His and NH3·H2O was 0.6–200 μM and 0.5–60 mg/L, with limits of detection of 0.5 μM and 0.2 mg/L, respectively. Furthermore, Ru-Flu@COF gel labels together with a smartphone enable portable, in situ and naked-eye monitoring freshness of pork, fish and shrimp, where the freshness level can be visually identified through the gel label color from red (fresh) to orange (less fresh), yellow (spoilage) and green (severe spoilage). The proposed smart label has the advantages of high sensitivity, excellent visual discrimination and portability, and it can be a promising platform for in situ nondestructive monitoring freshness of various kinds of meat for food safety inspection in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important public health issue, the control of food safety is of irreplaceable importance and urgency for the protection of public health, and has garnered global attention in recent years (Wu & Chen, 2018). Meat products serve as a vital source of nutrition for humanity. Nevertheless, due to microbial contamination and enzymatic reactions, meat inevitably deteriorates during storage, transportation, and processing, thereby posing challenges in terms of food waste and safety risks. Consequently, rapid, sensitive and effective detection of meat freshness is of great practical significance for the assurance of food safety (Khan et al., 2023). Due to the action of microorganisms and endogenous enzymes, biogenic amines (BAs) and volatile amines (VOAs) (e.g. histamine, spermine, trimethylamine and ammonia) are produced in spoiled food, which are important indicators for assessing food freshness (Hu, Ma, Zhang, Che, & Zhao, 2015). Beyond giving off odor and affecting the taste of food (Andre et al., 2022), their accumulation can lead to dizziness, nausea and even gastrointestinal toxicity (Chow, Lam, & Wong, 2013), thus threatening human health. To date, researchers have developed many effective techniques for detecting the levels of harmful amines in food, including high-performance liquid chromatography (Chang et al., 2018), mass spectrometry (Nei, Nakamura, Ishihara, Kimura, & Satomi, 2017), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Liu et al., 2023), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Mário Sousa Diniz, 2021). However, in the on-site or screening analysis of food freshness, these traditional methods are often constrained by destructive manipulation of the meat samples, large and expensive instruments and lengthy processing steps (Jiang, Ye, et al., 2022). Therefore, the development of rapid, sensitive, nondestructive and portable methods for real-time monitoring of food freshness is urgent to safeguard food safety.

Fluorescence sensing technology has great potential for real-time field analysis due to the advantages of low cost, simple instrumentation and fast response (Yuan et al., 2023). However, the current monochromatic fluorescent probes for monitoring biogenic amines essentially rely on a “turn-off” or “turn-on” response mechanism, which faces challenges in terms of sensitivity, visualization and portability in practical applications (Zhang, Chen, et al., 2020; Zhang, Wang, Geng, & Liu, 2019). Not only are they susceptible to interference, but changes in the brightness of a single color result in poor visual discrimination. In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescence system has an excellent self-calibration capability, which can somewhat reduce the intensity-based interferences caused by the environment and instruments, etc. (Jia et al., 2019). Nevertheless, most ratiometric fluorescence sensors are single-response to biogenic amines, with another fluorescence emission serving as an internal reference (Si et al., 2022). Apparently, dual-response ratiometric fluorescent probes with inverse intensity changes can achieve higher sensitivity and accuracy, with better color gradients and visualization due to their intrinsic signal amplification. However, due to limited research in this area, this is still a challenge. Besides, the application of powder sensing materials in real-time monitoring of food freshness has been constrained, due to the problems of recycling challenges (Zhu, Zhang, & Zhu, 2016).

As an emerging class of crystalline porous materials, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) possess fascinating properties such as regular structure, open pores, high stability and large surface area (Mi et al., 2023). It is worth noting that benefiting from their abundant active sites and desirable pore structure, COFs can serve as a multifunctional host to pH-sensitive fluorescein dye, such as sodium fluorescein (Flu), enabling excellent sensing of BAs (Liu et al., 2022). The homogeneous encapsulation of Flu not only reduces aggregation-induced quenching of the dye molecules, but also offers protection and stability, preventing dye leakage, and reducing background fluorescence (Wang, Zhao, & Yan, 2020). Meanwhile, the regular pores and large specific surface area of COFs facilitate the contact and molecular recognition between the probe and target, thereby increasing the detection sensitivity (Zhang, Wang, Xie, Geng, & Liu, 2019). Consequently, functionalized COFs materials are increasingly considered a type of promising material for the fabrication of fluorescent sensors for monitoring food freshness.

Therefore, the objective of this study is to construct a highly sensitive, nondestructive and visual sensor for fast monitoring of meat freshness. The novelty in this research was to combine a green fluorescent dye-functionalized COF (Flu@COF) with the red fluorescent Ru (bpy)3 2+, thus a dual-color Ru-Flu@COF ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed with excellent visual color discrimination (Fig. 1 ). In fact, the rationale is to modulate the fluorescence color of the dual-response system by combining the mechanism based on Flu@COF fluorescence enhancement due to structural changes and Ru (bpy)3 2+ attenuation due to the inner filter effect. Initially, the quantitative analysis and visual sensing capabilities of Ru-Flu@COF are discussed regarding the meat spoilage markers (e.g., model targets His and NH3·H2O). Subsequently, Ru-Flu@COF was integrated with agarose hydrogel to fabricate a portable smart tag, which, when coupled with a smartphone, enabled the successful real-time visual tracking of the freshness of pork, fish and shrimp. Finally, by comparing with the total volatile salt base nitrogen (TVB-N) values, a color card was established, which allows visual discrimination of the freshness status simply by observation of the gel label color change. This novel design strategy for building an intelligent in situ meat freshness sensing system will be useful for meat food safety inspection.

As a typical imine-based covalent organic framework, TpDq COF was synthesized using a rapid and efficient salt-mediated crystallization approach reported in the literature (Jia, Wang, Zhao, & Yan, 2022b). Briefly, a certain amount of 2,6-diaminoanthraquinone (DAAQ, 107 mg) and p-toluenesulfonic acid monohydrate (PTSA, 430 mg) catalysts were accurately weighed and then mixed with 50 μL of ultrapure water and stirred uniformly for 5 min. Afterwards, 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol (TFP, 63 mg) was added and stirred thoroughly for another 10 min. The obtained paste was then heated at 170 °C for 5 min under sealed conditions, and the resultant brownish-red powder was washed several times with hot water, DMA and ethanol respectively to remove the excess catalyst and unreacted monomer. Finally, TpDq COF was obtained by drying under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h with a yield of 82.3%.

Flu@COF was prepared through guest encapsulation referred to the literature with slight modifications (Ji et al., 2023). The freshly prepared TpDq COF powder (30 mg) was added to 5.0 mL of ethanolic solution of sodium fluorescein (0.05 M), followed by sonication for 10 min to make it well-dispersed. Next, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 80 °C for 5 h. To remove the excess dye adsorbed on the COF surface, the solid was washed several times with ethanol until the supernatant showed no fluorescence. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of BAs and VOAs
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 10 mg of Flu@COF powder was dispersed in 5.0 mL of deionized water and treated with ultrasonication for 30 min to obtain a well-dispersed suspension. Then 100 μL of this Flu@COF suspension, 100 μL of Ru (bpy)3 2+ solution and different volumes of amine solution were added sequentially into a centrifuge tube and fixed with deionized water to 1.0 mL (Ji et al., 2023). Finally, the fluorescence spectra were measured under the excitation at 280 nm with a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi) and the fluorescence images were photo-taken with a smartphone (vivo X60, China) under the UV light irradiation at 302 nm in a UV analyzer (ZF-8, Jiapeng Technology Co., Shanghai).

The hydrogel labels were prepared with reference to the literature with appropriate modifications as detailed in Section 1.3 and Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information (SI). Herein, 2.5 mL plastic tubes with caps were used as vessels for the gas phase reaction. Different concentrations of NH3·H2O target solutions were transferred into plastic tubes, and then covered with caps containing hydrogel materials for sensing in a sealed environment for 30 min (Jia et al., 2023). After the reaction was completed, the caps were removed, irradiated with 302 nm UV light, and continuous fluorescence color change images of the gels were acquired with a smartphone (vivo X60, China). Finally, the corresponding RGB values were extracted by the installed third-party color acquisition software (Color Identifier, China) for concentration analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for testing of meat freshness of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of the smart hydrogel tags in real sample monitoring, shrimp, pork and fish were purchased from a local supermarket for freshness test. Specifically, whole shrimp of moderate size and a slice of pork and fish samples of the same quality and similar shape along with hydrogel labels were placed in Petri dishes (Lu et al., 2023) (three parallel groups), sealed with cling film, then stored at 25 °C, 4 °C and −20 °C, protected from light, for 0–48 h, 0–72 h and 0–5 d, respectively (Li et al., 2023). During this period, the trends of meat spoilage and hydrogel fluorescent labels were continuously photographed and recorded under both daylight and UV light at 302 nm for colorimetry and fluorescence schemes, respectively. The TVB-N value was estimated by the Semi-micro Kjeldahl method (GB 5009.228–2016) (Chinese national standard) with some modifications (see the SI for details) (Goulas & Kontominas, 2005).

The successful preparation of TpDq COF and Flu@COF was confirmed by a series of characterizations. First, the microscopic morphology and structure of the material were observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM). As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, the morphology of TpDq COF presented nanofiber strips with about several microns, while the basic morphology of the material remained unchanged after Flu modification, with only a slight shortening of the nanofibers length (Jia, Wang, Zhao, & Yan, 2022a), suggesting the topological structure of the COF was basically preserved. From the powder X-ray crystal diffraction (PXRD) patterns in Fig. 2C, the synthesized COF showed characteristic peaks at 3.4°, 6.1°, 7.1° and 27°, corresponding to the (100), (110), (210) and (001) reflection planes, respectively, which were consistent with the simulated peaks (DeBlase, Silberstein, Truong, Abruna, & Dichtel, 2013), suggesting the successful synthesis of a crystal framework with AA stacking mode. Importantly, the crystal structure remained stable after Flu encapsulation. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. 2D showed that the N-H bands at 3423 and 3331 cm−1 corresponding to DAAQ in TpDq COF disappeared, and the intensity of the C=O vibrational peak at 1640 cm−1 of TFP was substantially weakened. Meanwhile, new C=C and C-N stretching bands appeared in TpDq COF at 1570 and 1255 cm−1, respectively (Jia et al., 2022b), confirming the formation of β-ketoenamine. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra of Flu@COF exhibited no significant changes, except for a new characteristic ArCOOR stretching band at 1390 cm−1 assigned to Flu (Wang, Roitberg, Meuse, & Gaigalas, 2001), indicating the successful encapsulation of Flu in TpDq COF without affecting the structure of pristine COF.

In addition, the surface chemistry and binding environment of the material were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in Fig. 2E, the XPS spectrum of Flu@COF showed a new peak accounting for Na (1s), demonstrating the presence of Flu on the material. The X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) energy spectra and elemental mappings of COF and Flu@COF in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 also demonstrated the existence of Na elements and their uniform distribution on Flu@COF. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the photoelectron peaks of C 1s, O 1s and N 1s ascribed from the keto-amine and enol-imine forms can be detected simultaneously in the TpDq COF structure (Li et al., 2020), with the ketoamine form dominating (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). Second, after modification of COF with Flu, the peak positions of N 1s in Flu@COF shifted to higher binding energies, while the C 1s and O 1s spectra shifted to lower binding energies, revealing the interaction between dye and COF (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the zeta potential of Flu@COF was significantly higher compared to that of COF due to the presence of Na+, indicating a possible electrostatic interaction between COF and Flu (Fig. S6) (Jia et al., 2023). The optical properties of the materials were investigated by ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis). Fig. 2F illustrated that the pure fluorescein sodium salt showed a significant absorption peak at 477 nm, while the absorption peak is red-shifted to 529 nm in Flu@COF. This red shift was attributed to the lower transition energy and larger electron delocalization of the conjugated system (Wang et al., 2020). And the characteristic absorption of Ru (bpy)3 2+ was located at 285 nm and 450 nm. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of COF and Flu@COF were 861 m2/g and 836 m2/g, respectively, with corresponding Type IV N2 adsorption isotherms (Fig. S7), demonstrating the mesoporous nature of the material. Apparently, the surface area of Flu@COF was slightly reduced due to the encapsulation of Flu molecules compared to plain COF. All these results demonstrate the successful binding and coordination interactions between Flu and COF. Finally, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves (Fig. S8) showed that they both retain their high structural stability at temperatures up to 400 °C, manifesting their favorable thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of amine-response ratiometric fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescent properties of the materials were investigated as shown in Fig. S9. The pure COF material was almost non-fluorescent, whereas the modified Flu@COF displayed a characteristic fluorescence emission at 521 nm, which underwent a pronounced blue shift and possessed lower background fluorescence compared to pure Flu. In addition, the fluorescence emission of Ru (bpy)3 2+ was situated at 602 nm, which is 80 nm apart from the maximum emission wavelengths of Flu@COF. Therefore, by combining green fluorescent Flu@COF and red fluorescent Ru (bpy)3 2+ (Fig. 3 A), a ratiometric fluorescence platform (Ru-Flu@COF) was constructed without any spectral overlap (Abbasi-Moayed, Bigdeli, & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2022). Taking biogenic amine (His) as a typical representative marker of food spoilage, when His was present in the Ru-Flu@COF system, the emission peak intensity of Flu@COF at 521 nm gradually increased, while the emission peak of Ru (bpy)3 2+ at 601 nm steadily decreased. This dual inverse change of the fluorescence signals achieved a gradient color change from red to green, giving it superior visualization (Fig. 3B). This color trend can be demonstrated in the fluorescent chromaticity diagram by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) as shown in Fig. 3C.

To further validate the detection feasibility of the Ru-Flu@COF probe for food freshness markers, five typical biogenic amines, including histamine, cadaverine, putrescine, spermine and tyramine, were investigated. The results illustrated that the Ru-Flu@COF probe displayed comparable ratiometric fluorescence responses to all five BAs (Fig. S10). Therefore, the Ru-Flu@COF probe is practical for visually monitoring the total BAs content in assessing food spoilage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of Ru-Flu@COF system for amine
ce:section.ce:para: The hazardous and reactive histamine (His) and the typical volatile amine (NH3·H2O) were selected as model targets to evaluate the quantitative analysis capability of amine solutions. Relevant experimental conditions were investigated prior to the investigation to achieve the best detection performance, including the synthesis ratio of Flu@COF, the concentration of Flu@COF and Ru (bpy)3 2+, excitation wavelength and sensing time (Figs. S11–S15).

Under the selected conditions, the quantitative analysis and visual sensing capabilities of Ru-Flu@COF towards His and NH3·H2O were studied as depicted in Fig. 3D and G. As the concentrations of His and NH3·H2O gradually increase, the peak intensity of Ru-Flu@COF at 521 nm progressively increased, while the peak at 601 nm correspondingly decreased, exhibiting a pronounced dual-response signal. The intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (F521/F601) exhibited a great linear relationship with the concentrations of His and NH3·H2O in the range of 0.6–200 μM and 0.5–60 mg/L (the standard curve was plotted using the least squares method), respectively (Fig. 3F and I). And the limit of detection (LOD) for fluorescence sensing of His and NH3·H2O, calculated from the regression equation, were 0.5 μM and 0.2 mg/L, respectively (the LOD was calculated using the results of regression analysis with the definition LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank signals of 11 trials and k corresponds to the slope of the linear regression equation of the calibration curve). Therefore, Ru-Flu@COF demonstrated high sensitivity to the His and NH3·H2O sensing, implying a great potential for sensitive tracking of food freshness. The PXRD peaks of Flu@COF remained nearly unchanged prior to and subsequent to the reaction with the target, signifying the stability of its crystal structure throughout the reaction process (Fig. S16). In addition, the increasing concentrations of His and NH3·H2O also lead to a rich color gradient transition of the Ru-Flu@COF probe from red to orange, then yellow, and ultimately to green under the UV lamp at 302 nm for excitation, as shown in the insets of Fig. 3E and H. This color variation was further confirmed by the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. S17), indicating the remarkable visual detection capability of the designed system for biogenic amines, even to the naked eye. Moreover, compared to the monochromatic fluorescence sensing using Flu@COF (Fig. S18), where the linear range and the LOD for His sensing were 5∼150 μM and 4 μM, respectively, the Ru-Flu@COF ratiometric fluorescence system presented a broader linear range, a lower LOD and superior visualization, suggesting that the incorporation of red-emitting Ru (bpy)3 2+ to construct a dual inverse fluorescence sensing system holds significant advantages in enhancing detection sensitivity and visualization capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual sensing of volatile organic amines (VOAs)
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve nondestructive, real-time monitoring of meat freshness, the sensor must be a “sniffing system” for amine vapor detection. Coincidentally, agarose hydrogel, known for its high loading capacity and low background fluorescence, is an ideal choice for the development of portable fluorescent probes (Abbasi-Moayed et al., 2022). In this work, the Ru-Flu@COF system was combined with agarose to further construct a portable hydrogel smart label for gas phase sensing of volatile amines, thus facilitating on-site assessment of food freshness (Fig. 4 A). As seen in the selectivity experiments (Fig. S19), the hydrogels exhibited a significant fluorescence color change from red to green in the presence of several volatile amines, while showing negligible response to common laboratory volatile reagents, demonstrating the high selectivity of the Ru-Flu@COF hydrogels for the visual detection of VOAs.

To further investigate the gas sensing performance, the typical volatile amine NH3·H2O was chosen for further investigation. The conditions of material ratio, thickness, sensing stability and response time of the gel were carefully investigated, respectively (Figs. S20–S23). Under these selected experimental conditions, the Ru-Flu@COF hydrogel tags exhibit sensitive, naked-eye visible gas-phase sensing capabilities towards NH3·H2O, with a gradual fluorescence color transition from red to orange-yellow, and eventually transitioned to yellow-green, as the NH3·H2O concentration increased from 0 mg/L to 3000 mg/L (Fig. 4B). To enhance the portability of the sensing platform, we have further integrated the hydrogel tag with a smartphone. By utilizing the smartphone camera and color analysis software, RGB values of the hydrogel can be directly extracted, favoring rapid on-site monitoring of meat freshness. It was observed that the G/R values exhibited a steady rise trend as the NH3·H2O concentration increased, demonstrating a strong linear correlation within the range of 10∼1000 mg/L, and a low LOD at 8 mg/L (Fig. 4C). These results fully validate the feasibility of combining Ru-Flu@COF hydrogel with a smartphone for accurate and visual sensing of volatile amines. Furthermore, the gel labels can be regenerated by acid solution treatment and pure water washing, retaining almost their original G/R values after two cycles, showing the reusable advantage of Ru-Flu@COF hydrogels for real-time monitoring (Fig. S24).

Notably, the Ru-Flu@COF sensing platform constructed in this work outperforms other methods for the detection of His and NH3·H2O, as listed in Table 1 . Significantly, the system not only possesses an extensive linear range and a low LOD, but the dual-color ratiometric fluorescent probes allows for a dynamic color transition from red to green, thus substantially enhancing the sensitivity of visual detection. Moreover, the prepared hydrogel sensor functions as an excellent “olfactory” system for sensing meat freshness with superior stability and recyclability, making it more advantageous and versatile compared to powder- and paper-based sensors. As a result, the potential for practical applications of the Ru-Flu@COF sensory hydrogel is extensive and promising. Finally, the introduction of a smartphone in the strategy further circumvents the need for large and expensive instruments, enhancing the cost-effectiveness and portability of this approach for practical applications.

To understand the mechanism of the Ru-Flu@COF probe for excellent reversible sensing signals towards the target amine, a series of studies were performed using histamine as a model. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the presence of His only caused a 2.2-fold increase in the green fluorescence intensity of Flu@COF and an 8.6% quenching in the red fluorescence of Ru (bpy)3 2+ (Fig. 5B); and in the Ru-Flu@COF system, the enhancement in Flu@COF intensity and the quenching efficiency of Ru (bpy)3 2+ increased to 3.7 times and 40%, respectively (Fig. 5C). Apparently, compared to the individual Flu@COF and Ru (bpy)3 2+, the Ru-Flu@COF probe in the hybrid system provides improved sensing and visualization of biogenic amines.

To delve deeper into the potential dual-response mechanism of the probe, the fluorescence lifetime of the system was first characterized. The test results revealed that, upon reaction with 50 μM His, the excited state lifetime of the Ru-Flu@COF hybrid probe at 521 nm significantly increased from 3.9 ns to 4.0 ns (Fig. S25A and Table S1) (Wang et al., 2021). The fluorescence enhancement of Flu is mainly due to the conversion of the phenolic hydroxyl group to a ketonic structure in the presence of basic amines, as well as the opening of the lactone ring structure to form deprotonated carboxyl groups (Fig. S26A) (Quan et al., 2021), which allows the molecule to change from a poorly luminescent ester structure to a dianionic form with high quantum yield (Liu et al., 2022), thus leading to a substantial enhancement of the green fluorescence, as confirmed by the redshift in the UV–Vis spectra of Flu (Fig. S26B). In contrast, the excited state lifetime of Ru (bpy)3 2+ at 601 nm exhibited a negligible change from 369 ns to 367 ns with the introduction of target His (Fig. S25B and Table S1), suggesting that the fluorescence quenching should be caused by the static quenching mechanism. However, as illustrated in Fig. S27, the continuous reduction in Ru (bpy)3 2+ fluorescence intensity was not directly attributed to the target His. Further exploration unveiled that the fluorescence excitation spectra of Flu@COF and Ru (bpy)3 2+ overlapped near 280 nm (Fig. S28A), leading to competitive absorption of excitation light. Therefore, upon reacting with the target His, the excitation spectrum of Flu@COF notably enhanced (Fig. S28B), influencing and weakening the fluorescence emission of Ru (bpy)3 2+. Additionally, the UV–Vis peak of Flu@COF also exhibited spectral overlap with the fluorescence emission peak of Ru (bpy)3 2+ in the range of 500∼700 nm (Fig. S28C). The UV–Vis absorption of Flu@COF gradually increased with the incorporation of the target His (Fig. S28C), which was another factor contributing to the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Ru (bpy)3 2+.

Different from other fluorescence platforms that only take Ru (bpy)3 2+ as a stable internal reference, the present study ingeniously transforms it into a BAs-responsive fluorescent indicator. Such a dual-response mechanism facilitates improved visualization and detection sensitivity. Overall, by combining the sensitive pH-responsive structural change-induced fluorescence enhancement strategy with the static quenching mechanism based on the BAs (Fig. 5D), the present Ru-Flu@COF probe achieves efficient fluorescence ratiometric determination and visualization of biogenic amines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time in-situ visual inspection of real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: In consideration of the feasibility and versatility of real-time sample monitoring, shrimp, fish and pork were selected for testing and validation in this work. Fig. 6 A illustrates the schematic diagram of the visual analysis of the real samples through the smartphone program. The prepared fluorescent hydrogel markers (three parallel sets) were placed in Petri dishes to prevent direct contact between the sample and the label (Fig. S29). To exclude the potential interference from the smart fluorescent hydrogel label row during sample detection, we first assessed the stability of the labels after storing them at different temperatures (25 °C, 4 °C and −20 °C) for 72 h. As depicted in Fig. S30, there was virtually no distinct change in the fluorescence intensity and color, confirming the strong stability and practical applicability of the prepared hydrogel labels. To demonstrate the validity and accuracy of this RGB method, the total volatile base nitrogen content (TVB-N) of shrimp at three different temperatures was measured using the Semi-micro Kjeldahl method. The relationship between shrimp freshness and TVB-N content is as follows: <12 mg/100 g for fresh, 12–20 mg/100 g for edible but less fresh, 20–25 mg/100 g for borderline, and >25 mg/100 g for inedible and spoiled (Jia et al., 2019). According to the Chinese national standard (GB2733-2015), shrimp products with TVB-N content exceeding 30 mg/100 g are not allowed for consumption or sale (Zhang, Dong, Chen, Gao, & Shi, 2022).

According to the experimental results (Fig. 6B–D), shrimp samples showed signs of less freshness after 4 h of storage at 25 °C, as indicated by the transition of fluorescent labels from red to orange, and the T-VBN content reached 15.7 mg/100 g. After 8 h, the fluorescence of the gel label shifted to yellowish green and the TVB-N content was 26.5 mg/100 g, illustrating the degree of deterioration further increased, rendering the sample inedible. After 12 h of storage, the fluorescence color of the label turned green, with the TVB-N content increasing to 36.5 mg/100 g, signifying severe spoilage. At 4 °C, shrimp spoilage occurred at a slightly slower pace, with minor deterioration observed after 24 h, resulting in a TVB-N level of 20.0 mg/100 g. After 72 h, the label turned completely green, and the TVB-N level reached 31.4 mg/100 g, indicating significant deterioration. Samples stored at −20 °C showed no signs of spoilage until 5 d, at which time the TVB-N content was only 16.1 mg/100 g. The deterioration rate of fish and pork was slightly slower than that of shrimp, but the overall trend for all three was similar as shown in Fig. S31.

The above results demonstrate that Ru-Flu@COF hydrogel labels can be effectively employed for in situ and non-destructive monitoring of freshness during the storage of shrimp, fish, and pork samples. Notably, with the degree of spoilage increases in these food items, the hydrogel labels exhibit a distinct color transition from red to green. Furthermore, the corresponding G/R values of shrimp samples at varying levels of food spoilage stored at different temperatures showed a great linear relationship (Fig. 6E) with their T-VBN contents (R2 = 0.97, 0.99 and 0.98, respectively), and this strongly confirms the precision and reliability of the Ru-Flu@COF hydrogel labels in assessing the meat freshness. Building upon the relationship between the fluorescent color change of the labels and the content of TVB-N in shrimps, a color card was also established (Zhang, Ma, et al., 2022), where red represents fresh, yellow denotes spoiled, and green signifies severe spoilage under 302 nm UV light (Fig. 6F). In practical applications, the on-site, non-destructive, and visual analysis of meat freshness can be readily achieved through simple color comparison, offering a convenient and effective means for freshness assessment. This work provides a novel strategy for the design and preparation of functionalized COFs-based hydrogel materials, which offer substantial value for real-time food safety monitoring and safeguarding public health. Furthermore, it holds the potential for monitoring the quality of a wider range of food categories in the future.

To conclude, a dual-color ratiometric fluorescent sensor (Ru-Flu@COF) was constructed to realize visual monitoring of meat freshness. Owing to the stimulus-response structural change, Flu@COF undergoes a sensitive fluorescence enhancement in response to alkaline amines, while Ru (bpy)3 2+ displays an opposite quenching response due to the inner filter effect. Leveraging this dual-color fluorescence inverse change, the Ru-Flu@COF sensor demonstrates high sensitivity, rich color gradients and good visual resolution for visual monitoring of meat freshness. The combination of Ru-Flu@COF-based gel labels with a smartphone enables a portable sensing platform for on-site and visual freshness analysis of meat samples, such as shrimp, fish and pork. Validation against the standard TVB-N freshness evaluation method confirms the accuracy and reliability of this sensor. However, it is difficult for this method to achieve rapid differentiation and accurate quantification of different biogenic amines. In the future, the integration of machine learning techniques may hold promise for further classification and quantification of different amine markers for monitoring meat freshness, and this new strategy may be expanded to explore methods for the monitoring of food spoilage of other kinds of food.

Zexi Zhou: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chenghui Li: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Juan He: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiandeng Hou: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22204110), the Ministry of Education of China through 111 Project (No. B17030), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China. We also thank Dr. Yunfei Tian, Yanju Luo from the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for the XPS and the fluorescence decay curves measurements.
